exemplar.title: An experiment investigating the effects of red and black text on short-term memory recall

detailedExemplarData.title: An experiment investigating the effects of red and black text on short-term memory recall

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clearly stated and wellâjustified aim with realâworld relevance
Hypotheses (null and alternative) include operationalised IV and DV
Theory/model link is explicit, with two frameworks connected to the study
weaknesses: Discussion of theories is somewhat brief and repetitive
Link between MSM and colour theory could be elaborated
Literature review could cite more recent empirical studies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both the aim and relevance are explained in depth, the variables are fully operationalised, and two theoretical frameworks are connected to the investigation. Minor stylistic repetition and slightly shallow theory elaboration preclude a perfect 6, so a holistic score of 5 reflects strong performance across all three strands.

strengths: All major controls and materials are identified and their role in validity is indicated
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are described
Design is correctly identified and its basic purpose is noted
weaknesses: Justification of design choices is minimal, lacking discussion of alternatives or order effects
Timing decisions (e.g., 3-minute reading) and pilot-study adjustments are not fully explained
Potential biases from a homogeneous teacher sample and researcher imbalance are not addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student lists design, sampling and controls, explanations are superficial and omit key justifications (e.g., rationale for timings, pilot changes, biases). This performance sits in band 1â2 for the Exploration criterion.

strengths: Appropriate descriptive and inferential statistics (means, SDs, MannâWhitney U) are applied
Findings are interpreted with reference to data and hypothesis
Calculations are broadly consistent with raw data
weaknesses: Minor transcription inconsistencies cast doubt on full accuracy
Graph contains scaling and labeling errors and conflated error bars
Effect size is omitted and p-value formatting is inconsistent
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The statistical approach is sound, and interpretation links results to the hypothesis. However, presentation errors, missing effectâsize measures and small calculation inconsistencies limit performance to the middle of the 3â4 band, meriting 3 marks.

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to background theory (Elliot et al. 2007, Goldstein)
Relevant strengths and limitations are identified and briefly linked to validity
Modifications are proposed based on stated limitations
weaknesses: Discussion is concise and could incorporate deeper theoretical mechanisms
Modifications are not explicitly mapped to each limitation
Some methodological limitations (e.g., fatigue, generalisability) are overlooked
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student integrates findings with theory and identifies appropriate strengths, limitations and future modifications, but explanations remain surface-level and links between limitations and changes are implicit, placing performance in the lower half of the 3â4 band.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart summarises means and standard deviations for each condition.  It addresses the hypothesis and includes clear labels; however, axes scaling is coarse, SD bars are conflated with separate columns, and the legend is vague.  These presentational faults align with âgraph addresses the hypothesis but contains errors,â meriting 3 marks within the 3-4 range.

The student states the statistical outcome (U, critical value, p) and directly interprets it in relation to the data and the research hypothesis, concluding that black text aids recall.  Interpretation is linked back to consistency (SD) as well.  Although phrasing could reference effect size, the criterion for fully linking findings to both data and hypothesis is met, deserving 5 marks (not 6 as some nuance is lacking).

Both a null and an alternative hypothesis are provided.  The Independent Variable (text colour: red vs black) and Dependent Variable (number of words correctly recalled) are explicitly named and operationalised; the DVâs measurement method is spelled out and the IVâs levels are concrete.  No ambiguity remains about how either variable is to be manipulated or scored, satisfying the highest rubric requirement, so a full 6 marks is justified.

Sampling is identified as a blend of purposive (teachers) and random selection within that frame.  The rationale â reducing bias and yielding a representative sample of the faculty â is provided, fulfilling the requirement for explanation.  However, limitations of this strategy are only briefly noted later, and no alternative techniques are weighed, so the work sits at the lower end of the 3-4 band (3 marks).

Two theoretical frameworks are described: the Multi-Store Model (MSM) to situate short-term memory and colour theories (Goldstein 1942; Elliot et al. 2007).  Each is summarised with essential detail and, crucially, the narrative explicitly links these ideas to the planned manipulation of text colour and expected effects on recall (e.g., red as "stimulating and forceful" potentially impairs cognition).  The linkage is clear but not deeply elaborated (e.g., MSM is named then quickly abandoned), so the strand fulfils the descriptor for band 5-6 but does not demonstrate exceptional depth required for the full 6 marks.  A score of 5 is therefore appropriate.

Findings are not only reiterated but discussed through comparison with Elliot et al. (2007) and Goldsteinâs colour theory, showing conceptual integration.  The student also reflects on real-world associations of red with danger.  While the discussion is concise and could incorporate additional sources, it surpasses mere description, placing the work in the 5-6 band; 5 marks recognise respectable but not exhaustive discussion.

The student clearly states an aim â "to determine whether reading text in different colours affects short-term memory recall in adults."  They then devote two paragraphs to why this is worth studying, linking colour-induced arousal to classroom, workplace and testing environments.  The explanation explicitly connects improved environmental design to potential real-world benefits, moving well beyond a mere identification of relevance.  Minor stylistic issues do not detract from a sound explanation; hence performance fits securely in the 5-6 band and merits 5 marks rather than the top of the range because the argument, while clear, remains brief and somewhat repetitive.

Several relevant strengths (colour-blind screening, quiet venue, mixed gender) and limitations (small, homogeneous teacher sample; short exposure) are identified and each is briefly explained in terms of impact on validity or generalisability, fulfilling the descriptor for band 3-4.  Explanations, however, remain surface-level, so a solid 4 (upper end of the band) is appropriate rather than entry into the top band.

Controlled variables (reading time, quiet room, font, language background, colour-blind screening) and materials (identical stories, answer sheets, timing devices) are listed and each control is linked to validity (e.g., minimising distraction).  This satisfies the rubricâs demand for explanation.  Discussion remains surface-level and omits justification for stimulus length or story content, so 3 marks within the 3-4 band are suitable.

The student proposes larger, more heterogeneous samples, additional colours, extended exposure and repeated trials.  These suggestions do stem from stated limitations (sample size, ecological validity) but links are implicit rather than explicitly mapped, and justification is quite general.  Hence the work satisfies âmodifications described but not explicitly linkedâ at the 3-4 level; the midpoint (3) accurately captures quality.

The design (independent measures with random allocation) is not merely listed but briefly explained as allowing a "clear comparison" and reducing selection bias.  While justification is concise, it goes beyond description, fitting the âexplainedâ criterion.  Depth is limited (no discussion of alternative designs or control of order effects), so the lower end of the 3-4 band (3 marks) is warranted.

Means, standard deviations and a Mann-Whitney U test are employed â appropriate choices for two independent, small, non-parametric groups.  Calculations appear internally consistent with the raw data and critical value.  Minor transcription inconsistencies (e.g., duplicated values in the appendix) cast slight doubt on complete accuracy, so the strand fits âappropriate but containing errors.â  The upper value of that band (4) recognises overall competence despite imperfections.

annotations.comment: The description of the Multi-Store Model is accurate but could be strengthened by detailing how sensory and long-term components relate to the colour manipulation in this study, clarifying the theoretical link.
Goldsteinâs (1942) colour theory is presented, but the student could update this foundation by referencing recent empirical studies on colour-induced arousal to bolster the literature review.
The summary of Eliot et al. (2007) omits key methodological details (sample size, context); including these would contextualise its relevance and strengthen the exploration of prior findings.

The transition from the Multi-Store Model to Goldsteinâs colour theory lacks an explicit connection; the student should explain how attention and arousal mechanisms interact conceptually with colour effects on memory.
The independent measures design is explained, but discussion of potential experimenter effects (e.g., different researchers per group) would deepen the justification for design choices.
While materials are described, the student should justify the selection of story content and length in terms of cognitive load theory to support materials validity.
While the IV and DV are identified, operational definitions could be integrated hereâfor example, specifying that ânumber of words correctly recalledâ is measured via list-recall accuracy.
The alternative hypothesis is non-directional; to align with the expectation that black text outperforms red, consider phrasing a directional hypothesis such as $$H_1: \mu_{black}>\mu_{red}$$.
Mathematical formatting would clarify hypothesis notationâuse $$H_0$$ and $$H_1$$ consistently for subscript clarity instead of manual tags.
The rationale for purposive and random sampling is stated; however, potential biases arising from a homogeneous teacher sample (e.g., similar cognitive profiles) should be evaluated.
Controls are listed well, yet the choice of 3-minute reading and 2-minute recall durations lacks justification; explain how these timings optimise measurement sensitivity.
The pilot studyâs feedback informed procedural adjustments, but the report should specify which changes were implemented and their impact on the main studyâs validity.
Table 1 headings are misaligned and use line breaks; reformat or consolidate header cells for clearer comparison of red and black conditions.
Procedure states differing numbers of researchers per room (one vs. two); discuss how this imbalance might introduce observer bias and how it was mitigated.
The text introduces means and SDs but should specify units (âwords recalledâ) to contextualise the statistical summary.
The non-parametric test rationale is sound but the use of a one-tailed test contradicts the non-directional hypothesis; reconsider test tailing or adjust hypothesis accordingly.
The p-value is reported to five decimal places; for clarity, round to two significant figures (e.g., p =.041) in line with APA conventions.
Reference to Figure 1 does not detail what each graphical element represents; directly label bars or include a legend to improve interpretability.
Error bars appear conflated with bar widths; separate standard deviation indicators visually and clarify in caption to address presentation errors.
The discussion effectively references Elliot et al. (2007) and Goldstein, but could elaborate on attentional narrowing mechanisms to deepen theoretical integration.
Colour-blind screening is a strength, yet the screening methodâs validity is not described; specify the test used to establish its reliability.
Future directions mention multiple trials but could detail a specific experimental design to examine long-term colour exposure effects on memory retention.
Statistical findings link to the hypothesis, yet effect size (e.g., $$r$$) is omitted; include an effect size measure to fully characterise the practical significance.
Limitations address sample size but overlook possible learning or fatigue effects between pilot and main trials; include this to present a comprehensive limitations review.
The section describes logistical delays (traffic) which are not central to hypothesis testing; refocus on methodological or design limitations for relevance.
Generalisation claim to similar target groups should be tempered, as mixed gender doesnât address other demographic or cognitive differences impacting external validity.
Proposed modifications list additional colours but lack explicit linkage to specific limitations; connect each suggestion directly to its corresponding limitation for full justification.
The conclusion restates findings but would benefit from specifying theoretical and practical implications, such as applications in educational material design.
